paper_title: CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: A key component of creativity is associative reasoning: the ability to draw novel yet meaningful connections between concepts. We introduce CREATE, a benchmark designed to evaluate models' capacity for creative associative reasoning. CREATE requires models to generate sets of paths connecting concepts in a model's parametric knowledge. Paths should have high specificity (distinctiveness and closeness of the concept connection) and high diversity (dissimilarity from other paths), and models are scored more highly if they produce a larger set of strong, diverse paths. This task shares demands of real creativity tasks like hypothesis generation, including an extremely large search space, but enables collection of a sizable benchmark with objective answer grading. Evaluation of frontier models shows that the strongest models achieve higher creative utility than others, with the high multiplicity of answers and complexity of the search making benchmark saturation difficult to achieve. Furthermore, our results illustrate that thinking models are not always more effective on our task, even with high token budgets. Recent approaches for creative prompting give some but limited additional improvement. CREATE provides a sandbox for developing new methods to improve models' capacity for associative creativity.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: A key component of creativity is associative reasoning: the ability to draw novel yet meaningful connections between concepts. We introduce CREATE, benchmark designed to evaluate models capacity for creative associative reasoning. CREATE requires models to generate sets of paths connecting concepts in models parametric knowledge. Paths should have high specificity (distinctiveness and closeness of the concept connection) and high diversity (dissimilarity from other paths), and models are scored more highly if they produce larger set of strong, diverse paths. This task shares demands of real creativity tasks like hypothesis generation, including an extremely large search space, but enables collection of sizable benchmark with objective answer grading. Evaluation of frontier models shows that the strongest models achieve higher creative utility than others, with the high multiplicity of answers and complexity of the search making benchmark saturation difficult to achieve. Furthermore, our results illustrate that thinking models are not always more effective on our task, even with high token budgets. Recent approaches for creative prompting give some but limited additional improvement. CREATE provides sandbox for developing new methods to improve models capacity for associative creativity. 1. Introduction Creativity, as one of the three pillars in Sternbergs Triarchic theory of intelligence (Sternberg, 1985) and the highest cognitive skill in Blooms Taxonomy (Bloom et al., 1964), is central to scientific discovery, writing, and creative problem 1New York University 2The University of Texas at Austin. Correspondence to: Manya Wadhwa <mw4141@nyu.edu>. Preprint. March 11, 2026. 1 Figure 1. Motivating example of brainstorming paths in knowledge graphs. In CREATE, only the question is given; reasoning over the graph is implicit in the models parameters and thinking trace, similar to drawing connections for scientific research. Finding strong, distinct paths can be challenging. solving. flurry of recent work aims to develop AI agents for these tasks; for example, for hypothesis generation (Liu et al., 2025), research idea generation (Guo et al., 2025), and the entire scientific process (Lu et al., 2024; Majumder et al., 2024; Mitchener et al., 2025; Agarwal et al., 2025; Si et al., 2026). But how do we know if our models are creative enough? Real-world, complex queries are challenging and subjective to evaluate (Chakrabarty et al., 2024; Wadhwa et al., 2025), while symbolic benchmarks using abstract tasks (Nagarajan et al., 2025; Schapiro et al., 2025) may not reflect how the models are used in reality. This work presents new benchmark for associative creativity, striking balance between real-world applicability and verifiability. We take motivation from combinatorial creativity (Boden, Margaret A., 1991), which requires the formation of new thoughts by linking, recombining, or drawing analogies across familiar concepts. This process closely aligns with what we term associative creativity and plays foundational role in creative reasoning. Prior research, from Koestlers bisociation (Koestler, 1964) to Gentners CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity structure-mapping theory (Gentner, 1983), demonstrates that many creative insights originate in the associative leaps characteristic of combinatorial creativity. Our work focuses on associations between concepts: being able to surface unobvious but striking connections between known concepts is crucial for new discoveries. We present CREATE, benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs ability to form open-ended associations among realworld entities. Figure 1 shows an example of query designed to test this kind of reasoning. CREATE is practical, as it relies on world knowledge about concepts and relations rather than synthetic scenarios; it also sits at the right level of complexity: it is simple enough to evaluate yet still requires multi-hop reasoning about real concepts. The queries in CREATE are constructed to span different conceptual domains: people in movies, people in positions of power, genes associated with certain diseases etc. By formulating questions in sufficiently broad manner, we can test whether LLMs can enumerate the creative, trivia-like connections that human domain expert might be aware of as salient and interesting. Figure 1s possible answers show diverse and interesting possible connections. Our setup involves reasoning about more open-ended question space compared to past work on multi-hop reasoning like HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) and following datasets (Trivedi et al., 2022; He et al., 2024; Ding et al., 2024). However, it has both objective and hard-to-find answers, unlike more brainstorming-oriented queries (Zhang et al., 2025b). Although this setting does not exhaust the full scope of combinatorial creativity, we believe limitations in LLM performance here meaningfully inform our understanding of LLM creativity more broadly. Our central goal is for LLMs to generate connections that (a) reflect close but non-typical associations between concepts and (b) that are diverse rather than simple variations on single theme. These criteria align with standard desiderata for creative products: high quality coupled with meaningful novelty. Following prior work (Zhang et al., 2025b), we capture both aspects with unified creative utility metric that integrates quality and diversity. Additionally, we also design distinctiveness metric to count strong, unique relations. Our results show that frontier models achieve high creativity utility scores on our benchmark, compared to other opensource models and are able to generate high quality and diverse connections. However, these models are still lacking in their ability to generate high distinctive solutions when compared to population of model responses. Our analysis also shows that spending more reasoning tokens does not necessarily lead to higher scores on our benchmark, raising questions about how current AI systems search conceptual spaces and what is required for them to genuinely support or participate in creative processes. 2. Background Tests for human creativity and diversity Human creativity is often assessed using tasks such as the Alternative Uses Task (Guilford et al., 1978), Divergent Association Task (Olson et al., 2021), and Remote Associates Test (Mednick & Halpern, 1968). While these are demanding for humans, taxing working memory and requiring the coordination of multiple concepts under cognitive constraints, they are comparatively easy for LLMs (Wenger & Kenett, 2025), which can exploit large context windows, retrieval tools, and parallel generation. In addition, standard test instances are often seen during pre-training, unlike for human participants. By contrast, our benchmark requires forming novel connections among real-world entities (Sebastian Vettel, DSG3 ) rather than familiar everyday objects (e.g., book, bottle, brick) that are commonly used in the Alternative Uses Task. Tests of model creativity Prior work proposes benchmarks (Ruan et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2025; Padmakumar et al., 2025; Fein et al., 2025; Guo et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2025), focusing on specific creative abilities like creative writing, scientific ideation and code generation. However, evaluation in these specialized domains is challenging where the use of LLM-as-a-judge for studies at scale can conflate quality, diversity and usefulness (Si et al., 2025). It is unclear how to scale human evaluation: even AI-created papers undergoing real review processes are later found to have major flaws (Liu, 2025). In contrast, Nagarajan et al. (2025) and Schapiro et al. (2025) introduce symbolic, highly controlled testbeds for probing combinatorial creativity and training models on abstract tasks. However, these settings are far removed from the complex, open-ended ways LLMs are used in real applications. Our work achieves the best of both worlds: we propose realistic, knowledge-grounded task involving real-world entities and concepts. By leveraging knowledge graphs and familiar domains, we expect LLM-as-a-judge evaluations to be comparatively reliable. Models are also criticized for returning homogeneous outputs (Zhang et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2025). We do not directly engage with distributional pluralism (Sorensen et al., 2024; Lake et al., 2025; West & Potts) in this work; however; it indicates that an ensemble of LLMs may face performance ceiling on our benchmark., and presents significant challenge for practical deployment (Anderson et al., 2024; Padmakumar & He, 2024). 3. Conceptual Framework 3.1. Defining and measuring associative creativity Define U(x) as set of possible outputs for some task x. For instance, if is mathematical theorem, U(x) is proof, if is writing prompt, then is story, etc. CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity We assume an LLM can produce set U(x) by repeatedly sampling, which we denote via πLLM(U p(x)) for prompt conditioned on the task. key feature of our tasks is that is likely to be of moderate size at most (10-100) with standard use of LLMs; an LLM must spend significant compute to identify high-quality items u, so enumerating large numbers of possible is challenging. We evaluate on two dimensions. First, quality measure : assigns score to each element of u. Second, distance measure : R0 defines distances between elements of U.1 We can combine these objectives into single metric following NoveltyBench (Zhang et al., 2025b). We define the creative utility of set as s(U ) = max τ (cid:88) i=1 γi1f (cid:0)uτ (i) (cid:1) min j<i d(cid:0)uτ (i), uτ (j) (cid:1) , (1) where τ denotes an ordering over . Items are scored via their incremental marginal utility under this ordering, which is function of how many items have already been selected (γ), their distance to previously selected items (d), and their quality . The first item is assumed to have distance of 1 from the empty set. γ defines user patience. Values close to 1 favor larger sets, which we believe reflects how LLMs would be used for challenging creative tasks in practice: user could scan through moderately-sized list of ideas.2 Our LLM systems return unordered sets , but s(U ) requires an ordering to be computed. We compute greedily optimal τi by finding the element uk that maximizes (uk) min j<i d(uk, uτj ) (2) Second, we consider separate measure of distinctiveness, intended to reflect more global or historical notion of novelty: whether path departs from common patterns in prior humanor model-generated outputs. For an output u, its distinctiveness is defined as the minimum distance to the universe of responses U(x). For collection of outputs, , we define the distinctiveness as the maximum value over elements of . 1We note that these criteria are explored in the theory of submodular functions (Lin & Bilmes, 2011) and in techniques like determinantal point processes (Kulesza & Taskar, 2012) 2This objective differs from NoveltyBench in two key ways. First, their notion of utility is discrete 0-1 rather than distancebased, which is not appropriate for our setting. Second, we eliminate their normalization term 1γ 1γU ; γ already discounts additional items and this terms tends to strongly penalize returning larger sets. ν(u) = min uU (x) d(u, u) ; ν(U ) = max uU ν(u). (3) This formulation reflects our goal of identifying at least one high-quality output that stands out from typical generations. Associative creativity on graphs This work focuses on associative creativity, which instantiates as paths in graphs. At high level, many creative insights come from linking concepts through structured relationships. For example, drug and disease may be connected through shared biological pathways or intermediate processes, rather than by single direct link. This abstraction provides simple way to study the associative part of combinatorial creativity. Concepts or entities in our graphs are nodes and relationships between them are edges. From this view, generating creative association means finding high-quality (strong) connection, and ideally one that is non-obvious. One distinguishing feature of this setting is that there are potentially large numbers of valid paths. However, quality is right-tailed: that is, there are elements of that are substantially more valuable than others, but which are hard to find. We can think of these as works of great literature, or great creative ideas, amidst large number of more conventional (but still well-executed) concepts. This differs from NoveltyBench, where many responses to prompt (Whats the best cart to get in 2023?) may be equivalent in quality. 3.2. CREATE Formalization We instantiate this framework in structured reasoning setting. Let = (E, R) be knowledge graph with entities and relations R. Paths are represented sequences of triples = (cid:2)(e1, r1, e2), (e2, r2, e3), . . . , (en, rn, en+1)(cid:3) where each triple (ei, ri, ei+1) consists of two entities ei, ei+1 connected by relation ri R. We say that path is structurally valid if consecutive triples share entities, i.e. the second entity of (ei, ri, ei+1) equals the first entity of (ei+1, ri+1, ei+2) (Note that LLMs may produce structurally invalid paths in their generations). We say that path is factual if the triples represent true relations. The universe U(x) consists of all structurally valid paths in connecting entities ei, ek and obeying the constraints of the prompt x. We evaluate factuality separately. Our prompts go beyond simple queries and instead ask about sets, as shown in Figure 1. We only consider structurally valid paths that satisfy the prompt given by x; in Figure 1, whether path successfully connects Dakota Johnson with fantasy or sci-fi movie. Quality in Graphs Our notion of quality is derived from the specificity of relations in the graph and their factual correctness. 3 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity Specificity measures how many entities could plausibly participate in given connection. Informally, we would think of Dakota Johnson as more connected to Antonio Banderas than she is to other actors; she has fewer step-fathers than co-stars. Being connected by being citizens of the same country is weak or less specific connection, unless it were particularly small country. This intuition suggests that what governs the specificity (or strength) of relationship is the number of entities typically in that relationship with another. More precisely, we define the quality of path as the specificity of its weakest triple. For an individual predicate-induced classes: triple (ei, ri, ei+1), we define two CA(ei, ri, ei+1) = { (x, ri, ei+1) is true }, CB(ei, ri, ei+1) = { (ei, ri, y) is true }. (6) where g() is monotonically decreasing function, so that triples participating in large, non-selective classes receive lower specificity scores. Factuality ensures that relations generated in exist in the graph. Let q(u) denote factuality function for path u, where q(u) = 1 if all triples in represent true relations. The quality (u) of path (from Section 3) is defined as (u) = I[q(u) = 1] min (ei,ri,ei+1)u σ(ei, ri, ei+1). This formulation favors paths composed of selective, informative relations and penalizes those with highly generic edges. Distance function We define the following distance function to be used in the measures in Section 3.1. Let path be represented by its string form str(u). We define the distance between two paths as transformation function of the cosine distance between their string representations. d(ui, uj) = g(1 cos(str(ui), str(uj))), (8) where, g(x) is cosine-annealed cosine distance (cid:0)1 cos (cid:0)π( 0.7 )2(cid:1)(cid:1) , 0 0.7, 0.7 < 1. (9) g(x) = 1 2 1, We use this transformation because we observed that paths with distances > 0.7 differed very substantially, with incidental overlap due to few shared tokens or concepts within 4 the domain. Paths with distances < 0.4 were almost always varying on very similar connections; we did not perceive any marginal value from these. Our function rescales the distance interval to account for these judgments; see plot and examples in Appendix B. 4. Constructing the CREATE Benchmark 4.1. Dataset Curation We use Wikidata to construct queries in CREATE. The data generation process is described in Algorithm 1 in Appendix A. We begin by manually selecting set of relationcategory pairs (r, c), each of which defines class Cr,c = (x, r, c) G. These pairs are chosen to ensure that the resulting classes are compact and semantically coherent, with members linked by specific role, condition, or function, for example, one (r, c) pair in our dataset is (cast member, Goodfells). Triples belonging to the corresponding class Cr,c, sampled from G, include [(Goodfells, cast member, Joe Pesci ), (Goodfellas, cast member, Vincent Gallo), (Goodfellas, cast member, Robert De Niro), . . ., (Goodfellas, cast member, Robbie Vinton)]. In the next step, we form unordered pairs from entities in Cr,c within the class. For each pair, we randomly select one of the two triples and sample informative outgoing edges from G, yielding an additional one-hop relation. For example, we create pair: [(Goodfellas, cast member, Robbie Vinton), (Goodfellas,cast member, Vincent Gallo)], and expand on the second triple to find the link: (Vincent Gallo, occupation, painter). We then create source path that links the three together in chain: [(Goodfellas, cast member, Robbie Vinton), (Goodfellas,cast member, Vincent Gallo), (Vincent Gallo, occupation, painter)]. The resulting query is formed using the head of the first triple and the tail entity of the last triple. We use an gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 to re-write the structured triple into natural language query of the form What is the connection between Robbie Vinton and someone who works as painter? We show additional examples in Table 3. Note, we use this source path as way to establish that there exists at least one strong path between the entities, but we evaluate model on its ability to generate multiple distinct and strong paths, as there can be many ways to connect these not necessarily in Wikidata. 4.2. Benchmark Statistics and Quality We select twelve relations (r) that span diverse domains. The starting entity in each query is primarily of the type people, genes, or chemical compounds, spanning both encyclopedic knowledge, classic trivia domains like movies, and scientific knowledge. In total we have 931 natural language queries, with at least one verified connection between CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity Figure 2. Examples of model-generated paths compared against population paths, along with quality scores and minimum distance values. The first and last connect artists through classic relations of directing, acting, performing, etc. The second path is the weakest according to the assessed specificity, because connection through St. Louis is potentially shared by many entities. the entities. Table 4 enumerates the list of relations, along with the number of queries for each relation and the number of unique starting entities. Examples of source paths and generated queries are given in Table 3. We describe the filtering process in detail in Appendix A. 4.3. Metric Operationalization Quality (u): We estimate triple specificity using single prompt in which large language model (gpt-oss-120b) jointly assesses the sizes of both predicate-induced classes and directly identifies the larger of the two. This estimated maximum class size is mapped to discrete score on fivepoint scale. Appendix provides more details. We evaluate factuality using an LLM-as-a-judge (gpt-oss-120b) on the relation level. The prompt for evaluating factuality is given in Appendix D.2. Distance d(ui, uj): The pairwise distance between paths, by getting their embeddings using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and using cosine distance as the function. The creative utility metric uses both Quality and Distance above, with patience factor that we vary in our analysis. Distinctiveness ν(u): We use responses from all models as our universe of responses. For each path u, we find the pairwise distance using the metric above, and then take the minimum distance as the distinctiveness value. We use all-MiniLM-L6-v2 as the embedding model for the paths. Human Evaluation We conduct human study to evaluate our triple specificity as metric and also use these annotations to validate the LLMs-as-a-judge prompt. Two authors on the paper, independently annotated 75 examples, scoring path on the same rubric as given to the LLM-asa-judge. The annotators achieve Pearson correlation of 0.70 between themselves and Pearson correlation of 0.67 between gpt-oss-120b ratings and average of the two annotator ratings. To validate the quality of the LLM factuality judgments, we first annotate set of 346 model generated relations and then validate gpt-oss-120b judgments against this set. The evaluator achieves an overall balanced accuracy of 85.9%. For the negative class i.e. incorrect relations, the evaluator achieves recall of 0.94 and precision of 0.52. For the positive class i.e. correct relations, the evaluator achieves precision of 0.98 and recall of 0.77. Appendix illustrates some examples of this validation study. 5. Case Study Does performing well on CREATE really require creativity? Our benchmark prioritizes paths that are high-quality and distinct from those that have already been found. We conduct an experiment where we investigate paths that are high-quality and distinct from the entire set of paths found across all models we evaluate. Details on the models are in Section 6. We identified examples by: (1) Filtering out paths that are invalid, factually inconsistent, or have specificity 3. (2) Selecting paths with high minimum distance to the population of paths from that query over all models. From the resulting (specificity, distance) scored pairs, we chose 3 representative samples. Table 7 shows the entire list of paths for query along with the corresponding closest paths from the population. Figure 2 shows these queries, the distinctive paths, and our analysis of them. The three examples all feature queries that have multiple possible multi-hop connections, requiring models to choose among different ways of linking the entity In each case, the selected path is to the target class. reasonably distinctive. We emphasize that in each of these cases, the path was only found by single model; other models in our sample did not retrieve it. Yet these are recognizable as interesting and novel connections! In this sense, the benchmark provides an opportunity for models to produce high quality, distinctive links over generic ones, and our scoring metrics will prefer such generations. The construction of these paths reflects classic features of creative reasoning under constraints (Boden, Margaret A., 1991). Each step must obey the rules of the task, meaning 5 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity that the system cannot invent new relations but must instead recombine existing ones in novel ways (which is feature of combinatorial creativity). Solving the task also involves an interplay between divergent and convergent thinking (Guilford, 1967): from Jean-Pierre Jeunet, the system must explore multiple outgoing relations, while simultaneously pruning most of these branches to retain only those that eventually reach an entity that has been nominated for the best animated film. As result, the query functions less as straightforward retrieval problem and more as structured creativity challenge. 6. Experimental Setup Models We evaluate diverse suite of both non-thinking and thinking large language models on the proposed task, covering broad range of architectures, sizes, and training paradigms. Non-thinking models include GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-4.1, Claude-3-Haiku, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct (Team, 2024), Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507(Team, 2025), and OLMo-3.1-32B-Instruct (Olmo et al., 2025). Thinking models include GPT-5-mini, GPT-5, Gemini-3-pro-preview, Claude-Haiku-4.5, Qwen3-32B (Team, 2025), and OLMo3.1-32B-Think (Olmo et al., 2025). Base prompt All models are evaluated using shared base prompt (Appendix E.1) (referred to as original in the results) designed to elicit diverse, high-quality relational connections. We prompt the model to generate multiple, possible candidates, so we end up with set of connections as the output. The model is instructed to output these multiple paths in the form JSON enclosed by <answer> tags. We then parse out the text into structured JSON to get all model generated paths. To ensure comparability across models, we use temperature of 0.7 and maximum generation length of 4096 tokens for non-thinking models. For thinking models we found this max length was too small, so we vary the token budget/available reasoning budget. For the APIbased model (e.g. GPT-5-mini), we vary the reasoning effort parameter across the supported settings (low, medium, and high). For the open-source models, we explicitly vary the reasoning budget (16k and 32k). Finally, we filter all paths to be structurally valid paths (Section 3) before computing results. Prompt Variations One potential approach to mitigating lack of creativity is the use of alternative prompting strategies. Prompt variations are commonly employed to steer model behavior and elicit alternative responses, for example, by paraphrasing the prompt or explicitly requesting different answer when the initial output is unsatisfactory (Zhang et al., 2025b). In our experiments, we examine whether such prompt-based interventions are effective in eliciting more diverse and higher-quality relational paths from large language models. (1) Be creative. We augment the base prompt with the instruction: Be creative in the type of relationships explored and generated. This variant directly encourages broader exploration of relation types. (2) Verbalized Sampling. We adopt verbalized sampling (Zhang et al., 2025a) to reduce mode collapse by having the model explicitly express probability distribution over its outputs. Detailed prompt is given in Appendix E.2. (3) In-context regeneration/iterative: After the initial generation, we query the model once again and explicitly ask the model to provide different answer while keeping all previous answers in the conversation context. This allows the model to see its previous responses and deliberately generate something new. The prompt for this is given in Appendix E.3. (4) Resampling: We obtain multiple independent generations from the model using the base prompt (Appendix E.1) with temperature set to 0.7. Each generation is independent and the model has no knowledge of previous generations. 7. Results 7.1. Creative Utility Table 1 reports performance of our different target LLMs on our benchmark. We compute creative utility at two different patience values, reflecting two different operating points in terms of how system results would be used (how quality is valued vs. quantity). We also report the average maximum quality of sets, average pairwise distance within set, number of paths that are valid and factually correct. Frontier models generally achieve the highest utility at both patience values. We observe that increases with number of paths, an effect that is amplified by higher patience values, which allows for more generations to contribute to the overall utility. However, this is not uniformly true; for instance, Gemini-3-pro obtains similar utility to GPT-5-mini (high reasoning) despite producing fewer paths, by having higher quality and more distinct paths. Figure 4 illustrates how utility changes with patience values. Differences become more pronounced as patience increases, owing to the inclusion of more paths in the metric. At patience of 1, GPT-5 continues being the best performing model. Claude on the other hand tends to be very conservative in the paths it generates, only generating paths when fact seems verifiable to it. Utility is also aligned with the observed maximum quality observed (max σ) and the average pairwise distance (avg d). Alternative prompting methods can lead to improvements depending on the model. Iterate and Resample interventions lead to the highest creative utility scores. Prompt-based interventions only weakly and inconsistently affect creativity across models Figure 3 illustrates how utility changes under alternative prompting strategies. Iterative and resampling methods are the most effective in increasing the utility, which is expected since these approaches introduce additional generation paths that directly contribute to the metric. Detailed results for this experiment are given in Table 15. For prompt variations, creative and original prompts perform very similar for models; they also tend to produce similar number of paths post validity and factuality filtering. verbalized prompting produces very low number of valid paths and factual paths. In Figure 4, we show how the utility values change based on the patience parameter. Figure 4. Creative utility vs patience for the frontier models, as well as prompt variations for GPT-5-mini. We see utility values being similar at lower patience but the difference increasing as the patience values increases strong quality and diversity, indicating the metric captures open-ended performance. Appendix shows examples of raw predictions from GPT-5, Gemini-3-Pro, and ClaudeHaiku-4.5, along with examples of paths with high and low quality quality scores (before factuality filtering). 7.2. Quality and Distinctiveness Following our initial analysis in Section 5, we perform an automated experiment to discern the distinctiveness of the generations. Using ν, we analyze the frontier of high-quality 7 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity Table 2. At the most lenient, Gemini-3-Pro has the highest utility. However, at the strictest, GPT-5 is able to balance the two metrics better than other models. responses produced by each model. partially undermines the effectiveness of this search. We subsample 300 queries from each model and, for each setting, filter out invalid paths, factual inconsistent paths, and paths with quality 3. For the remaining paths for each query, we calculate each paths distance from population of responses for the same query. Avg ν reflects the average distance to the closest path in the population, and max ν reflects the best produced instance for each query. Table 2 shows that distinctiveness values are similar across models. We particularly focus on prompt variations, and see that on average they do not produce very distinctive responses compared to the base prompt. Interestingly, iterate has the strongest positive impact here. This metric captures something different than utility: we see that resampling is less useful here, but knowing what model has generated before and explicitly instructing it to generate different things seems to find more distinct items. 7.3. Reasoning Trace Analysis Table 16 reports the number of relations, entities and triples explored by the open-source thinking models in their reasoning traces. We do not filter these for validity or factuality constraints. We extract these automatically by prompting GPT-4.1-mini using prompts in Appendix G.1. Increasing reasoning token budget doesnt necessarily increase the number of tokens used by the model; the number of entities explored is similar. Figure 9 shows sample reasoning trace from Qwen332B model. We see different strategies being used. For example, in the text highlighted in yellow, the model uses domain knowledge of the entity (acting and comedy). In text highlighted in orange, the model is trying to come up with non-obvious and more long tail connections, trying to make the leap to other domains like charity, sports. In the text highlighted in green, the model is using more of backward-chaining approach. Repetition of information 7.4. Quality vs Factuality In Table 8, we report creative utility without any factuality component of quality, along with the average number of factually correct triples generated by model. We observe tradeoff between utility and factuality: GPT-5 achieves lower utility (s) compared to Gemini-3-Pro, but has higher factuality. To quantify the utility-factuality tradeoff, we define factuality filter. We first define as the fraction of triples in path that are factually correct. We then vary and modify Equation 1 to compute factuality-adjusted utility: s(U, t) = max τ (cid:88) γi1 I(cid:2)q(cid:0)uτ (i) (cid:1) > t(cid:3) i=1 σ(cid:0)uτ (i) (cid:1) min j<i d(cid:0)uτ (i), uτ (j) (cid:1) , Note, instead of having unified quality metric as defined in Equation 7, we consider factuality to be another dimension for this analysis and use σ as the quality measure. Figure 5 shows consistent drop in creative utility the factuality cutoff increases, as expected. At the most lenient, when we consider all valid paths generate by the models, we observe Gemini-3-Pro achieving the highest creative utility, followed by GPT-5, then Qwen3-32B close behind. But, at the strictest setting, only retaining paths that are entirely factual, we observe GPT-5 performing the best, while opensource models have substantial drop, revealing large gap. Note that because many paths have 2 or 3 relations, the steepest drop-off is from threshold of 0.5 to 0.7. 8. Conclusion This paper presents benchmark for testing LLMs at associative creativity. Queries require models to find 8 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity set of strong, distinct connections between real concepts, mimicking the kind of associate creativity required more broadly, but maintaining evaluatability. Our results highlight strengths of current models, but also show that higher thinking effort is not necessarily silver bullet in these settings. Further work is needed to fully leverage LLMs for tasks requiring associative creativity.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the use of LLMs for creative pursuits like brainstorming scientific hypotheses. The view of the authors is that such systems are best employed to complement human creators; we envision this benchmark as driving better work on tools that will be useful in this fashion. However, we acknowledge that systems for automating creativity may be applied in other ways, including fully automatic pipelines for producing creative work. These raise the risk of several potential negative consequences, including decreasing the quality of creative products through AI offloading, reducing job satisfaction of human creators who feel compelled to use AI, economic impacts of jobs lost to such systems, and more. We believe that this paper advances creativity research in responsible way. First, by using an abstract task insulated from particular downstream application, we avoid the appearance that systems performing well on this task are ready to replace humans in particular domain. (Compare our work to, for instance, hypothetical benchmark of creativity for short fiction.) Second, by remaining rooted in principles from literature on creative theory, we avoid promulgating hype and try to strike measured tone about LLMs performance on these tasks.

Prompt D.1: Strength Prompt Goal: Given knowledge graph path consisting of multiple triples of the form (subject, predicate, object), estimate FOR EACH TRIPLE the number of members in the LARGER of two possible classes defined by the predicate. Formal Definitions (apply to each triple (s, p, o)): Define TWO classes. Class (Subject-variation class): {{ (x, p, o) is true }} This is the set of all possible SUBJECTS that could replace while keeping (p, o) fixed. The given subject counts as ONE MEMBER of this class. Class (Object-variation class): {{ (s, p, y) is true }} This is the set of all possible OBJECTS that could replace while keeping (s, p) fixed. The given object counts as ONE MEMBER of this class. Directional Discipline (CRITICAL): - You MUST evaluate both Class and Class independently for each triple. - NEVER collapse or reinterpret the relation. - For directional predicates (e.g., influenced, taught, founded, won, received): * Class asks: who/what stands in relation (predicate) TO the object? * Class asks: who/what the subject stands in relation (predicate) TO? - Always respect the original direction of the predicate. Procedure (apply to EACH triple in the path independently): 1. Identify Class from (p, o). 2. Identify Class from (s, p). 3. Use factual knowledge and reasonable estimation to estimate the size of EACH class. 4. Select the LARGER of the two estimated class sizes. 5. Explain your reasoning step by step for both classes. Tests for human creativity and diversity Human creativity is often assessed using tasks like Remote Associations Test (RAT) (Mednick & Halpern, 1968), Alternate Uses Task (AUT) (Guilford et al., 1978), and Diversity Association Task (DAT) (Olson et al., 2021). We describe the tests along with the evaluation criteria: Remote Associations Test (RAT) This test consists of three common stimulus words that appear to be unrelated. The subject must think of fourth word that is somehow related to each of the first three words. Alternate Uses Task (AUT) This test measures creativity of subject by asking them to list as many unique uses for common object (e.g. brick or paperclip) as possible. It evaluates four key areas generally, fluency, originality, flexibility and elaboration. In this work we only focus on originality (Wenger & Kenett, 2025). Diversity Association Task (DAT) This task involves naming ten nouns that differ as much as possible from each other (Wenger & Kenett, 2025). For RAT, we calculate the accuracy. For AUT and DAT we follow the originality calculations according to prior work (Wenger & Kenett, 2025). For AUT, originality is how novel the response is compared to the prompt. For DAT, originality is how different pair of words are in the response. For both of these tests, we use glove embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) and calculate cosine distance as measure of novelty. Since AUT only has 5 query words, we run each of these 10 times, to get an evaluation set of 50 instances. Similarly for DAT, we sample 50 responses from the model. Table 18 shows examples of instances for each of the tests along with model responses and the quantitative scores. In Table 17 we summarize the performance for GPT-4.1-mini and GPT-5-mini on these three benchmarks. We see that flagship reasoning model achieve very high accuracy on RAT. For the diversity benchmarks i.e. AUT and DAT, we see both models scoring high on originality. 17 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity Figure 9. Your task is to extract structured JSON object with two keys: relation and entities. ======================== ### 1. Output Format ======================== Return **only** single JSON object with this exact top-level structure: {{ relation: (relation1,relation2,...), entities: (entity1,entity2,...) }} No extra keys, no comments, no explanations, no text outside the JSON, and no trailing commas. ======================== ### 2. Key Definitions ======================== #### relation - An array of **strings**. - Each string is **relation / predicate phrase** that appears explicitly or is clearly implied in the trace, describing how two entities can be linked. - Include: - Verb/verb-phrase relations (e.g., worked at, served in, attended (alma mater)). - Noun-phrase relations that behave as connectors (e.g., commander-in-chief). - **Do not** include: - Full sentences. - Dates, numbers, or descriptions unrelated to relations. - Entity names. #### entities - An array of **strings**. - Each string is an **entity or concept** mentioned or implied in the reasoning trace. - Include: - People (e.g., Joe Biden, John Adams, Abigail Adams, George Washington). - Institutions and places (e.g., White House, U.S. Senate, National Archives). - Roles or collective bodies (e.g., U.S. Presidency, First Lady of the United States). - Abstract but named items (e.g., List of U.S. Presidents, Media portrayals (e.g., John Adams miniseries)). - **Do not** include: - Relations (those go into relation). - Dates, numbers, or purely descriptive adjectives. - Full sentences. ======================== ### 3. General Instructions ======================== 1. **Scan the entire reasoning trace** and extract: - All relation phrases relation - All entity names or conceptual entities entities 2. **Do not deduplicate** items within each list. Generate all entities and relations that appear in the trace as they appear in the trace. Do not remove duplicates. 3. Normalize entity and relation names to lowercase, remove extra spaces, and remove punctuation. 3. **Do not infer facts** that are not mentioned or clearly implied. 4. **Return only valid JSON**, with: - Double quotes around strings - Arrays for both keys - No other output ======================== ### 4. Input to process ======================== { trace } 30 CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity Table 17.
